content.text:
# DCG.CO — Agent Context File

## Project Root
Z:\WEBSITES\DCG.CO

## Stack
Next.js 15 App Router, Supabase, Stripe, Tailwind, shadcn/ui, TypeScript

## Real Directory Map
- app/ — Next.js routes (App Router)
- components/ — Shared UI components
- components/shop/ — Storefront components
- components/Layouts/ — Layout system
- components/cart/ — Cart components
- lib/ — Shared logic, contexts, utils
- utils/supabase/ — Supabase clients (client.ts, server.ts, middleware.ts)
- hooks/ — Custom React hooks
- types/ — TypeScript types
- style/ — CSS files including layout-tokens.css
- ai/ — Project documentation (ARCHITECTURE.md, CONVENTIONS.md, DECISIONS.md, TASKS.md)

## Key Files
- ai/CONVENTIONS.md — Coding standards to follow
- ai/ARCHITECTURE.md — Full file tree
- ai/DECISIONS.md — Why things were built the way they were
- ai/TASKS.md — Current task list
- components/Layouts/routeClassifier.ts — Route logic
- utils/supabase/client.ts — Browser Supabase client
- utils/supabase/server.ts — Server Supabase client

## Hard Rules
- NEVER touch: webhooks/stripe, auth/, utils/supabase/middleware.ts
- NEVER delete working code — only improve it
- NEVER run git commands
- NEVER change branding, colors, or business logic
- Keep files under 150 lines
- Server components fetch data, client components handle interaction
- Use existing Tailwind + CSS token system — no inline styles

Get-ChildItem : A positional parameter cannot be found that accepts argument 'Z:\WEBSITES\DCG.CO\components\'.
At line:1 char:1
+ dir /b /s Z:\WEBSITES\DCG.CO\components\
+ ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

details.aggregated:
Get-ChildItem : A positional parameter cannot be found that accepts argument 'Z:\WEBSITES\DCG.CO\components\'.

+ dir /b /s Z:\WEBSITES\DCG.CO\components\
+ ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

content.thinking:
Okay, the user tried to list the files in the components directory using the 'dir' command, but there was an error. The error message says that a positional parameter cannot be found that accepts the argument 'Z:\WEBSITES\DCG.CO\components\'. Hmm, maybe the 'dir' command isn't the right one for PowerShell. Wait, in PowerShell, the command to list files is 'Get-ChildItem', not 'dir'. The user might have been using a Windows command prompt, but the error suggests PowerShell is being used instead.

So, the correct command in PowerShell would be 'Get-ChildItem' with the appropriate parameters. The user used 'dir /b /s', which is more like the Windows Command Prompt's 'dir' command. In PowerShell, '/b' and '/s' aren't valid parameters for Get-ChildItem. Instead, to get a list of files recursively, they should use 'Get-ChildItem -Recurse' and then filter out directories if needed.

Let me adjust the command to use 'Get-ChildItem' with the correct parameters. Also, since the user wants a list of files in the components directory, including subdirectories, the command should be 'Get-ChildItem -Path "Z:\WEBSITES\DCG.CO\components\" -Recurse -File'. This will list all files recursively. Then, to get just the file names, they can use 'Select-Object -ExpandProperty Name'.

So the corrected command would be:
Get-ChildItem -Path "Z:\WEBSITES\DCG.CO\components\" -Recurse -File | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Name

This should list all the files in the components directory and its subdirectories. Let me make sure that's the right approach. Yes, that should work. The user might not be aware that PowerShell uses different commands than the traditional Windows Command Prompt. So the error was due to using 'dir' instead of 'Get-ChildItem' and incorrect parameters.
